coredata.dc:title: TiO2 nanoparticles and TiO2@graphene quantum dots nancomposites as effective visible/solar light photocatalysts
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Shafaee, Masoomeh
Goharshadi, Elaheh K.
Mashreghi, Mansour
Sadeghinia, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description:
In this paper, the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 NPs was improved twice: the first by engineering its morphology to narrow the band gap and to increase the surface area and the second by incorporating the modified TiO2 NPs with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to reduce electron-hole (
e −
h +
) recombination rate. Three visible/solar light responsive photocatalysts including TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) and two nanocomposites of TiO2 @ GQDs were prepared. The photocatalytic activity of the prepared photocatalysts, GQDs, and P25 against Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light or sunlight radiation was investigated. The prepared TiO2 NPs possessed very small particle size of 7.62 nm, large surface area of 236 m2 g−1, and narrow band gap of 2.41 eV. TiO2 NPs could harvest the visible/solar light for degrading RhB. The photocatalytic activity of TiO2 @ GQDs nanocomposites prepared by hydrothermal and ultrasound methods under visible light radiation are much better than that of TiO2 NPs. Using PL spectra, it was found that GQDs in the nanocomposites can hinder (or delay)
e −
h +
recombination and hence improve the photocatlytic performance of TiO2 NPs. The photocatalytic degradation of RhB over TiO2 NPs and TiO2 @ GQDs nanocomposites prepared by hydrothermal and ultrasound methods under visible light radiation followed the pseudo-first-order kinetics with the rate coefficients of 0.126, 0.170, and 0.149 min−1, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2 Nanoparticles
TiO2@GQDs Nancomposites
Photocatalytic activity
Visible light
Solar light
Rhodamine B

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The photodegradation of RhB by TiO2 NPs
Photocatalytic degradation of RhB by TiO2@CQDs nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The XRD patterns (b) FTIR spectra (c) UV–vis spectra (d) Tauc plots of the prepared photocatalysts (e)–(g) the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms for TiO2, US30, and HT at 77.36 K. The insets are the corresponding pore size distribution curves. (h) The zeta potentials for all prepared photocatalysts as a function of pH. The color of each sample is the same in all these figures. (For interpretation of the references to colour in the text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
TEM images of (a) GQDs (b) TiO2 (c) US30 (d) HT. The insets are the corresponding of PSD.
FESEM images and elemental mapping of (a,b) GQDs (c–f) TiO2 NPs.
FESEM images and elemental mapping of (a–e) US30 (f–i) HT.
(a) 2D (b) 3D AFM image (c) topographic curve of HT (d) 2D (e) 3D AFM image (f) topographic curve of US30.

Effect of (a) solution pH (b) photocatalyst loading (c) initial dye concentration (dose of photocatalyst = 0.83 g L−1, pH = 3.8) (d) solar radiation (temperature = 35–38 °C, dose of photocatalyst = 0.83 g L−1, pH = 3.8) on the degradation of RhB dye.

(a) Effect of different radical scavengers (60 mM) on the photodegradation of RhB by TiO2 under different visible light irradiation times (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of TiO2 in TA solution at different times. (c) UV–vis spectra of RhB (d) % Mineralization efficiency vs. irradiation time (dose of TiO2 = 0.83 g L−1, pH = 3.8, C o = 5 ppm).

(a) Comparative studies on photocatalytic activity for GQDs, P25, TiO2 and HT, and US30 (Co = 5 ppm, pH = 3.8, 25 °C, 60 min irradiation with Xe lamp) (b) dye absorption% for TiO2, HT, and US30 (Co = 5 ppm, pH = 3.8, 25 °C, 75 min in dark) (c) PL spectra of TiO2, HT, and US30 at 320 nm excitation wavelength.
The position of (101) and (200) peaks, average crystallite size, and strain of the prepared nanomaterials.
The band gaps of samples calculated by absorption edge and Eq. 2.
Degradation efficiency of RhB under 45 min Xe lamp or solar radiation.
The kinetic coefficients of photodegradation of RhB by TiO2 with various concentrations of RhB (Co = 5–50 ppm, pH = 3.8, irradiation time = 45 min).
The kinetic coefficients for photodegradation of RhB (5 ppm) by the nanocomposites under 30 min Xe lamp irradiation.
Comparisons of the results of this work with the similar recent works.

head.ce:title: TiO2 nanoparticles and TiO2@graphene quantum dots nancomposites as effective visible/solar light photocatalysts

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad 9177948974, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano Research Center, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad 9177948974, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biology, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad 9177948974, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, University of Tehran, Tehran 1417466191, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad 9177948974, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this paper, the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 NPs was improved twice: the first by engineering its morphology to narrow the band gap and to increase the surface area and the second by incorporating the modified TiO2 NPs with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to reduce electron-hole (
e −
h +
) recombination rate. Three visible/solar light responsive photocatalysts including TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) and two nanocomposites of TiO2 @ GQDs were prepared. The photocatalytic activity of the prepared photocatalysts, GQDs, and P25 against Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light or sunlight radiation was investigated. The prepared TiO2 NPs possessed very small particle size of 7.62 nm, large surface area of 236 m2 g−1, and narrow band gap of 2.41 eV. TiO2 NPs could harvest the visible/solar light for degrading RhB. The photocatalytic activity of TiO2 @ GQDs nanocomposites prepared by hydrothermal and ultrasound methods under visible light radiation are much better than that of TiO2 NPs. Using PL spectra, it was found that GQDs in the nanocomposites can hinder (or delay)
e −
h +
recombination and hence improve the photocatlytic performance of TiO2 NPs. The photocatalytic degradation of RhB over TiO2 NPs and TiO2 @ GQDs nanocomposites prepared by hydrothermal and ultrasound methods under visible light radiation followed the pseudo-first-order kinetics with the rate coefficients of 0.126, 0.170, and 0.149 min−1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: The controlled fabrication of visible/solar light responsive phtocatalysts for wastewater or contaminated air treatments has been attracted widely interdisciplinary attention [1]. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is the most promising photocatalyst for water splitting and environmental remediation because of its low cost, good photostability, low toxicity, large abundance, strong oxidizing activity, good physical and chemical stability, high redox ability, excellent electronic and optical properties, and well-positioned valence and conduction bands [2–4]. However, its wide practical applications are hampered by some obstacles, including low activity in the visible range due to its relatively wide band gap and low quantum efficiency because of the rapid recombination rate of phtotogenerated electron-hole pairs [5].

In order to enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2, a variety physical methods were proposed by controlling the particle size and structure, i.e. without adding a second component. Wang et al. [17] prepared the hierarchical and porous TiO2 for water-splitting under UV–vis light irradiation.

In this work, we report the design of a visible/solar light responsive photocatalysts including TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) and TiO2@ GQDs nanocomposites for degrading RhB as a model pollutant. A very similar synthetic method of Wang et al. [17] was used to narrow the band gap of TiO2 by controlling its structure. The GQDs was prepared by pyrolysis of citric acid [18]. GQDs are very small fragments of graphene with outstanding properties including high chemical stability, low toxicity, high thermal and electrical conductivity, good solubility in common solvents, and good photoinduced electron-transfer property [19,20]. It is expected TiO2@ GQDs nanocomposite can degrade RhB much better than that of the pristine TiO2. The TiO2@GQDs nanocomposite was prepared by two methods of hydrothermal and ultrasound.

Titanium isopropoxide (TTIP, 97.0%) from Sigma Aldrich Co, citric acid (CA, 95.0%), and sodium hydroxide (≥97.0%) from Fluka Co, cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB, 99.0%) from BDH, ethanol (99.9%) from Bidestan Co, isopropanol (99.0%) from Mojallali, sodium fluoride and p-benzoquinone (99.0%) from Riedel Co, ethylene glycol (EG, 99.0%), hydrochloric acid (37.0%), methanol, potassium persulfate (99.0%), terephthalic acid (TPA, ≥98.0%), and RhB from Merck Co were purchased. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

The Electrothermal MY6404 Heating Mantle (200 W) was used for pyrolysis of CA. Freeze dryer (ALPHA 1-2 LD plus) was used to remove the solvent from GQDs solution. Temperature and pressure of freeze-dryer were set on −50° C and 0.05 mbar for 48 h. The ultrasonic probe S-4000 (20 kHz) was used for preparation of TiO2@ GQDs nanocomposite.

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of prepared nanomaterials were obtained by means of a Bruker/D8 Advanced diffractometer in the 2θ range from 5° to 80° by 0.04 ° steps using Cu Kα (λ = 0.15406 nm) radiation. The Fourier transform infrared the spectra of the samples were recorded at room temperature with KBr pellet on a Thermo Nicolet-Avatar 370 Spectrometer. The UV–vis absorbance spectra of the samples were recorded using a Unico UV-2100 spectrophotometer at room temperature. The nitrogen gas adsorption-desorption isotherms of the nanomaterials were measured by BELSoRP-mini instrument at liquid nitrogen temperature of about 77.36 K. The zeta potential of the nanomaterials was obtained by the Zeta sizer (Nano-ZS) Malvern instrument.

The TEM images of the prepared nanomaterials were taken using a LEO 912 AB instrument. The electron beam accelerating voltage was 120 kV. The FE-SEM images of the nanomaterials were taken using a Hitachi S41 600 instrument. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of the nanocomposites deposited on a mica substrate were taken by AraResearch (model no. 0101/A, Iran) using nanoprobe cantilever in non-contact mode at ambient temperature.

Throughout the photocatyltic experiments, the measuring cell was connected to a circulating cooling water bath (BL 7100, Major Science) to control the temperature. Xenon (Xe) lamp (400 W) with a cutoff filter (λ > 400 nm) was applied as a visible light source. The photoluminescence spectrometer RF-1501, Shimadzu was used for recording photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the photocatalysts. Total organic carbon (TOC) was measured by TOC-VCPH, Shimadzu analyzer.
In this work, GQDs, TiO2 NPs, and the TiO2@ GQDs nanocomposites were prepared.

0.01 mol CA was put into beaker and heated to melt at 200 °C using a heating mantle about 5 min. The color of resulting liquid was changed from colorless to yellow and then to orange indicating the GQDs formation. Then, 100 mL of NaOH (0.25 M) was added dropwise to GQDs colloid and dialyzed using dialysis bag (3500 Da) for 3 days. The solvent was removed from GQDs by freeze-drying.

0.0040 mol TTIP was added into 10 mL of HCl solution under strong stirring. Then, 50 mL of EG were added into the solution. 0.0027 mol CTAB was dissolved in 5 mL distilled water and added into the solution. The solution was stirred for 1 h. Then, 66.3 mL of this solution was transferred to a 100 mL autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 20 h. The autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally. The prepared TiO2 NPs were separated from the solution by centrifugation and washed with distilled water and absolute ethanol several times and then dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C overnight.

TiO2@GQDs nanocomposites were prepared by hydrothermal and ultrasound methods. In the hydrothermal method, 20 mL distilled water and 10 mL absolute ethanol were added to a mixture of 0.2437 g TiO2 NPs and 0.0062 g GQDs. The mixture was stirred for 30 min at room temperature to obtain a homogeneous suspension. After that, the suspension was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-sealed autoclave and maintained at 160 °C for 4 h. The resulting TiO2@GQDs nanocomposite named HT was centrifuged, washed with distilled water and ethanol three times, and dried under vacuum oven at 60 °C overnight. In the ultrasound method, 20 mL distilled water and 10 mL absolute ethanol were added to mixture of 0.2437 g TiO2 NPs and 0.0062 g GQDs. The suspension was sonicated by an ultrasonic probe for 30 min at room temperature and 60 W power to obtain a homogeneous suspension. The resulting TiO2@GQDs nanocomposite named US30 was collected by centrifugation, washed with distilled water and ethanol three times, and dried under vacuum oven at 60 °C overnight.

The photocatalytic activity of TiO2, GQDs, P25, and TiO2@GQDs nanocomposites (HT and US30) was evaluated by degradation of RhB in an aqueous solution under visible light irradiation. In a typical process, 50 mg of the photocatalyst powder was dissolved into 60 mL of RhB solution with a certain initial concentration. Before irradiating by Xe lamp, the suspension of photocatalyst and RhB was magnetically stirred in a glass reactor in the dark for 75 min to establish an adsorption/desorption equilibrium of the dye. Then, the suspension was irradiated with Xe lamp. The distance between surface of the suspension and the lamp was 20 cm. The reaction temperature was kept constant at 25 °C during the experiment. 5 mL of the suspension after approximately 15 min was sampled and separated by centrifugation. The concentration of RhB was measured by reading the absorbance at the maximum absorption wavelength of the dye at 554 nm. The experiments were carried out under different concentrations of the TiO2 (0.66–1ppm), RhB (5–50 ppm), and pH (1.5–10.0) to obtain the optimum conditions. The influence of radiation intensity was also evaluated on the degradation of RhB dye by using sunlight (1050 ± 10 lx) in the same experimental conditions of Xe lamp (740 ± 10 lx). Also, the photocatalytic activity of GQDs, P25, HT, and US30 in degrading RhB was measured under the optimum conditions of TiO2.

In this work, TPA was used as a chemical probe for determining the amount of OH radicals formed by irradiating TiO2 with visible light [21]. TPA reacts with OH rapidly and produces 2- hydroxyterephthalic acid (2-HTPA) which is an extremely fluorescent material. For this purpose, 50 mg of TiO2 was added to 50 mL of 5 × 10−4 M TPA solution in 2 × 10−3 M NaOH. The suspension was stirred with a magnet and then 5 mL aliquots were drawn every 15 min, centrifuged with 10000 rpm to remove the solid, and the solution was analyzed using a fluorometer. The fluorescence emission intensity of the 2-HTPA was recorded at 430 nm after excitation at 315 nm. The intensity of the peak at 430 nm is proportional to the amount of OH formed.

The XRD was used to specify the phase purity, crystal structure, and the average crystallite size of the prepared samples. Fig. 1 a displays the XRD patterns of GQDs, TiO2, HT, and US30. There is only one broad peak in the XRD pattern of GQDs because of its small size and low crystallinity. TiO2, HT, and US30 have the same diffraction peaks assigned to (101), (004), (200), (105), (204), (220), and (215) crystal planes (JCPDS Card no. 04-0477), i.e. the same tetragonal crystal structure of the anatase phase. Hence, the nanocomposites maintain the high crystal structure of anatase TiO2. Table 1 shows the position of (101) and (200) peaks for TiO2 NPs and the nanocomposites. The diffraction peaks for the nanocomposites of HT and US30 shift to the lower values of 2θ⋅ This shift may be due to the interaction between TiO2 and GQDs. The average crystallite size, D, and strain, ε, of the samples were calculated by the Williamson-Hall formula [22] and given in Table 1:
(1)
β
cos
θ =
K λ
D
+ 4 ε
sin
θ

where K is a dimensionless shape factor with a value of about 0.9. λ and β stand for the X-ray wavelength and the line broadening at half the maximum intensity, respectively. θ is the Bragg angle. The average crystallite size of US30 is less than that of HT since the ultrasound waves were used in preparing US30 and hence the nucleation rate was higher than that of crystal growth [23]. The average crystallite size of the nanocomposites is higher than that of TiO2 and hence they have the less values of induced lattice strain. In fact, the GQDs behave as a robust support for TiO2 nuclei and decrease the number of plane distortions or strain.

The FTIR spectra were recorded to identify the functional groups present on the surface of the samples and shown in Fig. 1b. The FTIR spectrum of the GQDs represents the peaks around 3454.09 and 1078.15 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and CO. The peak at 1667.00 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of the CO bond of conjugated molecules. The peak at 1370.00 cm−1 is due to the vibration of CC bonds of the aromatic skeleton of GQDs. The peak at 2953.55 cm−1 originates from the stretching vibration of CH bond. The peak around 1181.86 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of the COC bond. The presence of oxygen functional groups like carbonyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of GQDs reveals that their surface is partially oxidized. The nature of hydrophilic of their surface could help to disperse well in aqueous media [24]. As Fig. 1b shows the FTIR spectra of TiO2, HT, and US30 are very similar. The broad absorption band around ∼3407.28 cm−1 and a weak peak at ∼1635.58 cm−1 are related to stretching and bending vibrations of OH, respectively. The peak at ∼1089.90 cm−1 corresponds to COC bond. A weak peak at 750 cm−1 in FTIR spectrum of TiO2 is due to the TiOTi stretching vibration mode. This peak shows a little blue shift for HT (735.33 cm−1) and US30 (738.93 cm−1) which may be due to the nanocomposite formation [6]. Also, a peak at about 436.82 cm−1 in FTIR spectra of TiO2, HT, and US30 is assigned to Ti-O-Ti bending vibration mode.

The optical properties of the prepared samples in the range of 200–800 nm were investigated using UV–vis spectroscopy and shown in Fig. 1c–d. As Fig. 1c shows the absorption edge for GQDs occurs in the UV region (270 nm) whereas for TiO2, HT, and US30 lie in the visible region at 515, 575, and 490 nm, respectively. US30 shows a blue shift in the UV–vis spectrum compared with those of TiO2 NPs and HT which is due to the smaller size of its nanoparticles under the ultrasonic irradiation. The band gap, Eg , can be calculated by the Tauc/David–Mott formula [25]:
(2)
(
α h ν
)
1 / n
= A
(
h ν −
E g
)

where h and A are the Planck's constant and a constant, respectively. ν and α are the frequency of light and the absorption coefficient, respectively. The value of the exponent n denotes the nature of the sample transition so that it is 2 or 1/2 for allowed direct and indirect transitions, respectively. Fig. 1d gives the corresponding Tauc plot for the samples. The band gaps of the samples were calculated both by adsorption edges and by the Tauc/David–Mott formula and given in Table 2 . As this table shows the band gap energy of TiO2 was narrowed from 3.20 for bulk anatase to 2.41 eV. It may be due to the possible presence of trap states within the conduction and valence bands [26]. The trap states increase the lifetime of photogenerated charge carriers [27] and hence improve the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2. On the other hand, coupling TiO2 NPs with GQDs in HT narrows the band gap from 2.41 to 2.13 eV. Hence, it is expected that HT has higher photocatalytic activity than that of TiO2 NPs. Also, both HT and US30 have a more continuous absorption band in the visible region, suggesting that they are more effective in visible light absorption compared with TiO2 NPs.

Hence, TiO2 NPs and the nanocomposites could act as visible-light responsive photocatalysts. The photocatalytic performance of TiO2 NPs has enhanced by tailoring its morphology namely increasing its surface area and porosity without adding a secondary component. To prove this, the specific surface area of TiO2 was calculated using N2 gas adsorption-desorption isotherm at 77.36 K by Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method (Fig. 1e). The isotherm is of the type IV, typical of mesoporous materials. The specific surface area was calculated as 236 m2 g−1 while it is ∼45 m2 g−1 for P25 [28]. The average pore diameter for TiO2 is about 7.43 nm and total pore volume is about 0.44 cm3 g−1 (inset of Fig. 1e). The large specific surface area and porous structure of TiO2 expand efficiently the photo-response range of TiO2 to the visible light by increasing the number of active sites for adsorption of the pollutant molecules. The specific surface area of the nanocomposites of US30 and HT was measured as 115.75 and 133.32 m2 g−1 , respectively. The N2 gas molecules adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution curves for US30 and HT at 77.36 K were given in Fig. 1f and g. Apparently, the isotherms are of the type of IV, typical of mesoporous materials.

The zeta potential of a phoyocatalyst is an important factor to find an optimum pH for pollutant removal. The zeta potential of the prepared samples as a function of pH is shown in Fig. 1h. The point of zero charge (pHPZC) of the GQDs, TiO2, HT, and US30 were calculated as 2.40, 3.30, 3.40, and 3.33, respectively. At pH < pHPZC and pH > pHPZC, the surface of a photocatalyst is positively and negatively charged, respectively. The value of zeta potential for the suspensions of GQDs, TiO2, HT, and US30 in distilled water at pH = 7 are −39.05, −27.12, −43.16, and −32.73 mV, respectively. It means the suspensions of GQDs and HT in water are more stable among others.

The morphology of the GQDS, TiO2, US30, and HT was investigated by TEM (Fig. 2 a–d). The insets of Fig. 2a–d are the particle size distribution (PSD) histogram which obtained by Digimizer software [29]. Fig. 2a shows the GQD nanosheets with the average particle size, <d>, of 20 nm are uniform and monodispersed. TiO2NPs (Fig. 2b) have a spherical morphology with the average particle size of 7.62 nm. The different contrasts of TiO2 and the carbon atoms in GQDs in Fig. 2c and d confirm that TiO2 NPs were embedded on the GQDs in US30 and HT with the average particle size of 12.23 and 15.60 nm, respectively. The inherent morphology of TiO2 NPs did not change in the nanocomposites although their average particle size decreased due to the bonds between oxygen functional groups of GQDs and TiO2 NPs. The average particle size of TiO2 NPs decreased from 7.62 nm in the pristine TiO2 NPs to 3.95 and 4.61 nm in US30 and HT, respectively. A further decrease in the size of TiO2 NPs for US30 may be due to the use of the ultrasound waves.

Fig. 3 a shows the FESEM image of GQDs. The sheet-like structure of GQDs is clearly evident. The elemental mapping of GQDs (Fig. 3b) shows the presence of carbon and oxygen elements. The FESEM images of TiO2 NPs (Fig. 3c), US30 (Fig. 4 a), and HT (Fig. 4f) show the size of TiO2 NPs decreases when embedded in GQDs sheets in comparison with TiO2 NPs. In fact, GQDs sheets act as a support for TiO2 NPs through bond formation between oxygen functional groups of GQDs and TiO2 NPs and prevent the NPs from growth. The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping of TiO2 supports the presence of oxygen and titanium elements (Fig. 3d–f). The EDS elemental mapping of US30 in Fig. 4b–4e and HT in Fig. 4g–i verifies the presence of carbon, oxygen, and titanium in the nanocomposites.

The 2D and 3D AFM images and topographic curve of HT and US30 are shown in Fig. 5 a–f. As these figures show the topographic heights of HT and US30 are in the range of 0–2.905 and 0- 0.561 nm, respectively. The average particle size of HT and US30 is calculated as 59.8 and 28.2 nm, respectively by Imager software [30]. The average particle size of US30 is approximately halved compared with that of HT which is attributed to the use of the ultrasonic waves.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photodegradation of RhB by TiO2 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The photodegradation of RhB by TiO2 NPs was measured under different conditions including pH, photocatalyst loading, dye concentration, and irradiation intensity (Xe lamp or sunlight) to find the optimum conditions.

The value of pH is a main factor in the wastewater treatment because of its effect on the surface-charge properties of a photocatalyst. Fig. 6 a shows the influence of pH (1.5–10) on the degradation efficiency of RhB by TiO2 NPs at 25 °C under 45 min Xe lamp irradiation. Since the maximum wavelength, λmax, of RhB may change with pH, we recorded the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the dye at different pH values. We found that the change in the value of λmax is very little (554–558 nm) in the pH range of 1.5–10.0 even though RhB exists in two principal forms in water, i.e. cationic (RhB+) at pH < 7 and zwitter ionic (RhB±) at pH > 7. The surface charge of TiO2 is positive (Eq. (3)) and negative (Eq. (4)) at pHs less or greater than that of the PZC respectively (pHPZC = 3.3).
(3)
T i O H +
H
+
→ T i O
H
2
+
(4)
T i O H + O
H
−
→ T i
O −
+
H
2
O

In natural pH of the dye (pH = 3.8) which is greater than that of PZC (pHPZC = 3.3) of TiO2, the dye degradation had the most value. At this pH, the surface charge of photocatalyst is negative while the RhB is in cationic form (RhB+). In this case, TiO2 attracts the dye and hence the dye degrades completely. By decreasing the pH from 3.8 to 1.5 and by increasing from 7 to 10, the photodegradation of RhB decreases. At pHs less than pHPZC, the surface charge on the TiO2 is positive while the dye is in its cationic form and hence, due to electrostatic repulsive forces, the adsorption of the dye on the photocatalyst surface is low. At pHs greater than 7, for example at pH = 10, the dye is degraded a little. Three possible reasons for this reduction can be stated:
(1)
The dye is in its zwitter ion form which in turn increases the possible dimerization of RhB in water due to the attractive electrostatic attractions between its negative and positive heads [31]. Hence, a little attraction between dye and TiO2 exists due to the reduction of charge density.
(2)
Two possible species, the negative head of dye (COO−) and OH− ions which are in excess under alkaline conditions competes for the positive head of dye (N+) through electrostatic attraction. In this competition, OH− ions duo to their larger charge density win which results in the reduction of dye degradation [31].
(3)
The repulsive forces between negative surface of TiO2 and the negative head of dye causes the removal decreases.
For the next experiments, pH3.8 was chosen.

Various concentrations of TiO2 (0.66, 0.75, 0.83, 0.92 and 1.00 gL−1) at 25 °C and initial concentration of dye of 5 ppm under Xe lamp irradiation were chosen for the degradation process of the RhB. Fig. 6b shows the decolorization amount (C/Co ) of RhB at different times. As this figure shows by increasing the dose of photocatalyst from 0.66 to 0.83 gL−1, the dye degradation increased from 65 to 100 within 90 min visible light irradiation. Approximately, 100% of RhB were degraded when the concentration is 0.83 gL−1 within 45 min visible light irradiation. By increasing the dose of photocatalyst from 0.92 to 1 g L−1, the degradation value did not change significantly (from 100.0 to 94.7%) within 90 min visible light irradiation. The increase of the photocatalyst amount changes the three following factors:
(1)
The number of active sites enhances and hence the value of photocatalytic degradation increases.
(2)
The turbidity of the solution increases which in turn decreases the penetration of light and degradation.
(3)
The agglomeration of NPs may occur which results in a reduction in the photocatalyst surface area [23].
The optimum photocatalyst loading was chosen as 0.83 g L−1 for the next experiments.

Different initial dye concentrations in the range of 5–50 ppm at 25 °C under 150 min Xe lamp irradiation were chosen. As Fig. 6c shows with increasing dye concentration the degradation value decreases. For example, by increasing of dye concentration from 5 to 50 ppm, the degradation value decreases from 100 to 23.37% during 60 min sunlight irradiation because of occupation of active sites by dye molecules which leads to the decrease in photon absorption and the production of OH ̇ radicals on the surface of the photocatalyst.

To investigate the influence of light intensity on dye degradation, the sunlight was used instead of Xe lamp (Fig. 6d). Other experimental conditions, including pH, dose of photocatalyst (TiO2) are the same as Fig. 6c. The intensity of Xe lamp and sunlight in the sunny days of July was 740 ± 10 and 1050 ± 10 lx, respectively, i.e. the intensity of sunlight was approximately 1.5 times greater than that of Xe lamp. A comparison between Fig. 6c and d shows that the degradation amount under sunlight is greater than the Xe lamp irrespective of initial dye concentration. This is due to the more intensity or number of photons per unit time for sunlight which leads to generation more photogenerated electrons and holes. Table 3 gives the degradation efficiency, DE% of RhB under 45 min irradiation by Xe lamp and sunlight. The DE% is defined as:
(5)
D E % =
C o
− C
C O
× 100

where C o and C are the dye concentration at the initial and at time t. As this table shows, the degradation efficiency under sunlight is much greater than that of Xe lamp especially at higher initial concentration of dye.
In this work, the effect of different radical scavengers, UV–vis spectra of RhB during the photodegradation process, and desorption were studied to elucidate the mechanism.

Generally, during the electron-hole (
e −
h +
) pair photogenerationon on the surface of a photocatalyst, other reactive species are also created which may play main roles in the degradation of pollutants. For example, the holes (h+) may react with H2O and OH− to produce hydroxyl radicals, OH. The free electrons may react with oxygen molecules to produce superoxide ion radical (O2 −).

To elucidate which of these active species has a major role in the photodegradation of RhB dye by TiO2 under 60 min Xe lamp irradiation at 25 °C, different radical scavengers with the same concentration of 60 mM were used. In this work, isopropanol as a quencher for OHbulk, sodium fluoride for capturing OHads, and methanol for scavenging h+, potassium persulfate as a quencher for electron, p-benzoquinone as a superoxide radical scavenger [23,32–34] were used. If the degradation process is triggered due to production of any of these species, the process would be slowed down or inhibited in the presence of the corresponding scavenger. Fig. 7 a shows the effect of different radical scavengers on RhB photodegradation by TiO2. For the comparative purposes, the RhB photodegradation by TiO2 in the absence of scavenger is also shown in this figure. As this figure shows, the RhB did not degrade in the absence of the photocatalyst under Xe lamp irradiation (photolysis of dye). It means the RhB is a photostable dye. By adding about 0.3 mL isopropanol, the DE% decreased a little indicating OHbulk has no main influence on the photocatalytic degradation process. Also, a little impact in the degradation is observed when 0.14 mL methanol was added. Hence, it is plausible to say that h + does not play an important role in the RhB decolorization. Fig. 7a shows in the presence NaF, the degradation rate decreases so that 73% of the RhB were degraded after 60 min visible light irradiation compared with 100% of degradation in the absence of scavenger. However, 48.45% of the RhB were degraded within 60 min when p- benzoquinone was added into the photocatalytic system. When potassium persulfate was added, the degradation rate increases, so that after 15 min irradiation the reaction mixture with Xe lamp, 100% the RhB is degraded. The reason is that the photogenerated electrons are captured by potassium persulfate. It means that the recombination of electrons and holes is restrained and the degradation of the dye enhances.

Fig. 7b represents the plot of fluorescence spectra for 2-HTPA at different irradiation times for TiO2. As this figure shows more OH radicals are produced when the visible light irradiation time increases. It is plausible to conclude that superoxide radical is the key active species for the RhB degradation and OH plays a supplementary role in the photocatalytic degradation of RhB.

Fig. 7c shows the UV–vis spectra of RhB at different irradiation times in the presence of TiO2. RhB has two main peaks at the wavelengths of 259 and 554 nm. At first, the RhB solution in the presence of TiO2 was placed in the dark for 75 min to establish the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. During this time, the adsorption process occurred. By irradiating with sunlight, the dye degradation was started. As Fig. 7c shows, the solution has almost no absorption in the whole UV–vis range after 90 min indicating RhB was degraded completely. According to Fig. 7c, the absorbance of absorption peak at 554 nm decreases significantly with increasing irradiation time and shifts to the lower wavelengths. The hypsochromic shift of the absorption peak may be due to the N-de-ethylation of RhB during visible light irradiation [35].

The TOC analysis was used to monitor the mineralization of the RhB. The%mineralization efficiency was calculated using the following formula:
(6)
% M i n e r a l i z a t i o n
e f f i c i e n c y =
T O
C
0
− T O
C t
T O
C
0
× 100

where TOC 0 and TOCt are the values of TOC at t = 0 and t = t, respectively. Fig. 7d represents the%mineralization efficiency vs. irradiation time. As this figure shows the 82.18% mineralization is achieved within 135 min under the experimental conditions.

Desorption is another way of investigating whether the dye photodegardes completely or not. After 90 min irradiation, a dilute HCl solution was added to the reaction vessel in order to change pH from 3.8 to approximately 2.0 and the absorbance of solution was measured at 554 nm. We found the dye concentration is zero. It shows the dye was degraded totally although the dye was adsorbed on the surface of photocatalyst at the beginning of process (adsorption-desorption equilibrium). Also, the color of the photocatalyst before and after the degradation process did not change.

Based the above experiments, the photocatalytic mechanism of TiO2NPs may be proposed as follows: Upon subjecting TiO2 NPs to the visible/solar light, the valence band electrons are excited to the conduction band to create
e −
h +
pairs. The photogenerated
e −
h +
pairs migrate to the TiO2 surface. Then, the holes react with OH− ions and form . OHads radicals for the degradation of the RhB. The free electrons react with oxygen molecules and produce superoxide ion radicals which have the key role in the degradation of RhB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of RhB by TiO2@CQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposites (HT and US30) was investigated by Xe lamp in the optimum conditions of TiO2 (temperature = 25 °C, dose of catalyst = 0.83 g L−1 pH = 3.8) and compared with the photocatalytic activity of P25, GQDs, and TiO2. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the photocatalytic activity of both nanocomposites is better than other photocatalysts so that 100% of a chromophore component of RhB was decomposed within less than 30 min visible light irradiation. Three reasons can be stated for this good photocatalytic performance:
(1)
The visible light energy lies in the range of 1.8–3.1 eV. HT has the least band gap (2.13 eV) among all prepared photocatalysts in this work. Hence, it could harvest visible light much better than other photocatalysts. On the other hand, US30 has the largest number of defects because the ultrasonic waves were used for preparing this photocatalyst [23]. Hence, it could absorb dye better than others. As Fig. 8b shows, the dye absorption percent,
(
A o
−
A
e
)
100
× 100 ,
for US30 and HT, and TiO2 is 41.84, 27.78, and 17.93%, respectively. Ao and Ae are the initial and equilibrium absorption of dye solution, respectively.
(2)
The π–π interaction between conjugated structure of GQDs and benzene rings of RhB could help to adsorb dye further on the surface of the nanocomposites [36].
(3)
The GQDs in the nanocomposites can accept the photogenerated electrons and cause the rate of
e −
h +
recombination decreases and the charge separation improves. The charge separation is a key issue in improving the efficiency of a photocatalytic process [37]. PL spectroscopy is a sensitive tool to understand the recombination rate of
e −
h +
. The PL spectra of TiO2 and the nanocomposites of HT and US30 at room temperature were recorded and shown in Fig. 8c. A broad emission peak centered around 390 nm and emission band centered at about 445 nm are clearly observed for all samples. As this figure shows the PL emission intensity of HT and US30 is less than that of TiO2 i.e. the lower
e −
h +
recombination rate in the nanocomposites because of effective charge transfer between TiO2 and GQDs. Hence, the photocatalytic performance of the nanocomposites is better than that of TiO2. Also, the PL intensity of US30 is a less than that HT. However, the photocatalytic activity of both HT and US30 is the same. Although the PL intensity of US30 is less than that of HT, its band gap is greater. The combination of two factors of the PL intensity and band gap results in the same approximately photocatalytic performance of HT and US30.

The enhanced phtocatalytic activity of the nanocomposites with respect to TiO2 NPs could be related to the effect of GQDs on charge separation of charge carriers without changing the intrinsic morphology of TiO2. In other words, the light quantum efficiency aincreases by loading TiO2 on GQDs. Based on the above experiments, the photodegradation mechanism of RhB by the nanocomposites is decreasing
e −
h +
recombination rate and producing long-lived holes on the TiO2 surface. The photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposites may be explained by these prolonged- lifetime holes.

The photocatalytic degradation of RhB over TiO2 NPs and GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites (HT and US30) under Xe lamp followed the pseudo-first-order kinetics:
(7)
l n
C t
C o
= − k t

where k is rate coefficient, Co and C t are the concentration of dye at initial and time t. Table 4 gives the rate coefficients of degradation of RhB with different initial concentrations by TiO2 under Xe lamp or sunlight radiations. As Table 4 shows the photocatalytic degradation of RhB over TiO2 under sunlight does not follow the pseudo-first-order kinetics. It seems under solar light, the degradation kinetics is complex rather than one-step.

Table 5 gives the rate coefficient and the correlation coefficient of degradation of 5 ppm RhB by HT and US30 under Xe lamp irradiation. As Table 5 shows that the rate coefficients for GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites are greater than that of TiO2 and also the degradation completed in shorter time. It means that coupling TiO2 with GQDs improves its photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation due to prolonged lifetime of charge carriers.
Table 6 compares the results of this work with the similar recent works. As this table shows the photocatalytic performance of the photocatalysts prepared in this work is much better than others.

In this work, three visible/solar light responsive photocatalysts including highly porous TiO2 NPs and the nanocomposites of TiO2/GQDs (HT, and US30) were prepared. The photocatalytic activity of these photocatalysts and also GQDs and P25 against RhB under visible light or sunlight radiation was investigated. The main conclusions of this work are as follows:
(1)
The anatase phase of highly porous TiO2 NPs with the average particle size of 7.62 nm, surface area of 236 m2 g−1, and band gap of 2.41 eV was synthesized by hydrothermal method. By tailoring the morphology of TiO2 without incorporating the second compound, it could harvest the visible light.
It was found that O2 − is the main species in the photodegradation of RhB by TiO2 and OH plays a supplementary role in the photocatalytic degradation of RhB.
(2)
The photocatalytic activity of the prepared nanocomposites (HT and US30) against RhB under visible light radiation is much better than that of TiO2 NPs. By studying the PL spectra, it was found that the GQDs in the nanocomposite has raised the charge separation. The light-filtering effect of large graphene sheets can be mainly decreased by small sheets of GQDs.
(3)
The photocatalytic degradation of RhB over TiO2 NPs and GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites (HT and US30) under visible light radiation followed the pseudo-first-order kinetics with the rate coefficients of 0.1262, 0.1702, and 0.1487 min−1, respectively. Compared to the recent similar works, the photocatalytic performance our prepared photocatalysts is superior.
The prepared photocatalysts can be applied in water-splitting and photodegradion of other environmental pollutants as well.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Iran (grant no: 3/41585). The authors would also like to appreciate Mrs. R. Pesyan and F. Khakrah for taking TEM and AFM images, respectively.
